results.title: New-ish player looking for alternatives to Weaver : r/Guildwars2
results.content:
For example, check out this Tempest build. Here's a link to the open world version, which is actually really hard to kill. It's also pretty simple to play, as you stay in fire attunement most of the time. Even if you just hit all your skills off cooldown you'll do pretty well.

The one thing I'd add is that if you're in need of some extra healing or condition cleansing you can also make use of water attunement. You get regen when you swap to water, you get a heal when you use water overload and weapon 3, and if you use your elite skill in water attunement you get a golem that heals you and cleanses conditions. So you have all of those healing options on top of the already great survivability of the regular fire gameplay. [...] Stone Resonance is kind of my panic button when I might be about to die to give me a quick shield and gain some time to both use my healing skill and maybe attune to water for a second.

Signet of Fire (Air, Water, Earth) is just there, you can use it if you want to get some more burning going on your enemies but they should usually already burn very much.

As for gear, I'm fully equipped with celestial gear head to toe but it took me a while to get that so something else might be a good substitute for the moment but some more clever person would have to help you there. I don't know anymore what I used back then. [...] Also I play Sword/Focus just because I like the skills the most. Sword is very mobile which I like a lot in open world content to get out of situations or into them faster. Fire 2 gives you a forward jump, water 2 jumps back and out of combat and gives you a heal wich is nice, Air 2 jumps to a target thats even further away than fire does and earth 2 gives you a free evade. Focus has Fire Shield and Flame Wall wich I like to use for more burning and Obsidian Skin as a potential panic button because it makes you invulnerable for quite a long time but as a panic button it gets a few more clicks to reach. But feel free to use whatever off hand weapon you like the most.

results.title: Dreamweaver alternative for Linux - Software & Applications - Spiceworks Community
results.content:
To reiterate whats been asked… Why you need it?

Dream weaver can be mocked up with notepad+﻿+﻿ Or really any dev kit on linux. You could write it in gedit for all intents and purposes really.

VIM…? :smiley:

:smiley:

VIM…? :smiley:

:smiley:

Done it.

Works great in a pinch for quick remote page fixes.

Aptana studio is great! It has HTML preview, PHP / Javascript debugging and FTP uploading

i was looking for it but like you said abandonware, i gonna give a try with notepad+﻿+﻿  
thanks for recommendation.

Google Chrome or Firefox for webdev not Adobe Dreamweaver. [...] # Dreamweaver alternative for Linux

Hello world!

It’s there any alternative IDE tool like Adobe Dreamweaver in Linux for web development?

Please recommendation.

I recommend Adobe Dreamweaver CS5.5, yes, on linux.

Nifty thing on linux called wine, I’ve done it before, works very well in fact.

Wine + Dreamweaver

\Edit: there are other options if you want out of the box such as Bluefish, but then again, how many people can say you’ve run a windows app on your Linux box? #Nerd

Why not use notepad++ ?

Question is, what you like about Dreamweaver? What do you use it for?

If it’s just to edit the source code, Notepad+﻿+﻿ is great. If you want to deal with having all your files in one place, look at Git. If you need SFTP access, look at using public/private key pairs. [...] Google Chrome or Firefox for webdev not Adobe Dreamweaver.

I’ve been enjoying ﻿ GitHub’s Atom as an alternative to the classic Notepad+﻿+﻿. I’ve only used the Windows version but there’s a Red Hat and Ubuntu version that may be worth looking into, and you can do cool things with packages like run a development server in your text editor .

sounds good!

VIM…? :smiley:

:smiley:

“Built with vi”  
“Best viewed in lynx”

Check out NVU. Works on PC, Mac and Linux. I don’t think it’s in development anymore, but the last release works pretty well. It is WYSWYG and it’s free.

Not sure why people are dodging the browser, using Chrome or Firefox gives you all the possible modern web dev tools you could possibly require…for free and cross system complaint…

results.title: Rapidweaver Alternative? - General Discussion - Blocs Website Builder Forum
results.content: You want a blog and editable content. You certainly need Volt CMS. This is hands down the best no-code CMS I’ve ever used, I’ve used five in the past. With RW/Stacks Alloy you could only use one blog and editable content needed to be done with droplets/embed process. You don’t need to use the latter with Volt, it is similar in use to WebYep, you just add a box on the page with a unique name to be web editable. Blogs you can have as many as you want with Volt, you are not limited to one. I created a website that has multiple sections using multiple blogs, it therefore became a website with all user editable pages. [...] In Oct 24 I tried Blocs, after about a week of experimenting was pretty much away with it. For a RW/Stacks user it is intuitive, similar overall concept but different in a number of ways and approaches has different ways of doing things, not massively different, but things such as setting breakpoints the order you make changes to cascade down to smaller screen layouts. [...] To use it could be best described as being a cross between Foundry and Foundation. You have the Foundry ease of the modular Bootstrap elements and you apply styling and functionality using classes similar to Foundation. Using the classes is great, I think the thing that makes them sound scary is that they are called classes, this can imply that you are getting in dirty with CSS and off putting for a no coder workflow (before you start using them). Foundry did the same but instead of calling it classes, it called it Blacksmith. Don’t think of it as classes, rename it in your head to custom options or something like that as it is all menu driven, but each class is it’s own personal options panel that can be applied individually to elements or to many elements for gobal control.

results.title: 7 open source alternatives to Dreamweaver | Opensource.com
results.content:
## 6. Wordpress

The open source content management service (CMS) and blogging platform, Wordpress, features a structured yet flexible page layout interface. It lets you create blog posts and pages with just enough flexibility to let your creativity thrive, and with just enough restriction to ensure that your creation renders correctly in all browsers. With Wordpress, you can have choose your desired level of complexity at every step. You can self-host it using their famous 5-minute install method, or you can buy hosting from Wordpress.com. You can use the drag-and-drop designer to create pages, or you can hack on HTML and PHP yourself. You can choose a contributed theme or you can design and create your own. It's a great resource for anyone who wants to run a website. [...] ## 5. Aloha Editor

Aloha Editor is a JavaScript-based WYSIWYG HTML5 editor that allows users to edit content in the same layout that readers view it. This is a pretty unique model, as it's not exactly an application itself, but embeds an editor into your HTML page. It requires a Node.js stack, so if you're not a developer familiar with Javascript then it can be difficult to configure. However, if you're a site admin looking for an easy editing interface for your users to make quick updates to their pages, then you should say "aloha" to Aloha.

## 6. Wordpress [...] ## 4. SeaMonkey

SeaMonkey is a community continuation of what was once a Mozilla-produced internet application suite. While Mozilla decided to narrow its focus to individual projects, SeaMonkey continues to make regular releases of its full suite, which includes SeaMonkey Composer, a simple WYSIWYG HTML editor. You'd struggle to do advanced layouts with Composer (for instance, you can't adjust the CSS display or property to create a two column text and image pair, but would have to use a non-responsive table instead), but for basic pages with zero code written, this is a realistic option.

## 5. Aloha Editor
